---
title: "2a Activity: Git and GitHub"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    css: _materials.css
    include-after-body: _progress.html
---


## Goal

We will create a brand-new practice repository on GitHub, open it in Codespaces, make one change, commit it, push it, and mark a shared version with a tag.

Note: The checkboxes on this page are only stored locally in your browser. They are not submitted or synced anywhere.

::: {.guide-progress}

Progress: <span data-progress-count>0</span> / <span data-progress-total>0</span> steps complete.

<button class="reset-progress" type="button" data-progress-reset>Reset progress</button>

:::


## Activity

::: {.panel-tabset}

## 1. Create A Repository

Start on GitHub in Chrome or Edge.

1. Go to <https://github.com>.
1. Click **+** in the upper-right corner.
1. Choose **New repository**.
1. Name the repository `python_tmr_practice`.
1. Choose **Private**.
1. Check **Add a README file**.
1. Click **Create repository**.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-create-repo">
  <span>I created a new private GitHub repository with a README.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-not-course-repo">
  <span>I confirmed this is a new practice repository, not my fork of the course repository.</span>
</label>

:::


## 2. Open it in Codespaces

From the new repository page:

1. Click the green **Code** button.
1. Click the **Codespaces** tab.
1. Click **Create codespace on main**.
1. Wait for VS Code Web to open.

If GitHub asks whether you trust the authors of the files, choose the "Yes" option.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-open-codespace">
  <span>I opened the new practice repository in Codespaces.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-open-source-control">
  <span>I opened Source Control in VS Code Web.</span>
</label>

:::


## 3. Make One Change

Open `README.md`.

Add a short research-project note, such as:

```markdown
## Project Notes

This repository is a practice project for learning Git and GitHub.
```

Save the file.

Return to Source Control and click the changed file to review the diff.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-edit-readme">
  <span>I edited and saved `README.md`.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-review-diff">
  <span>I reviewed the diff in Source Control.</span>
</label>

:::

Useful terminal reference:

```bash
git status --short
git diff
```


## 4. Commit the Change

In Source Control:

1. Find `README.md` under **Changes**.
1. Hover over `README.md` and click the **+** button to stage it.
1. Confirm that `README.md` moved from **Changes** to **Staged Changes**.
1. Write a descriptive commit message in the message box.
1. Click **Commit**.

If you have multiple changed files, stage only the files that belong together in this commit.

A good message for this practice change might be:

```text
Added project notes to README.
```

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-stage-readme">
  <span>I staged `README.md`.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-write-message">
  <span>I wrote a descriptive commit message.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-commit-change">
  <span>I committed the change.</span>
</label>

:::

Useful terminal reference:

```bash
git add README.md
git commit -m "Added project notes to README."
git log --oneline --max-count=5
```


## 5. Push To GitHub

In VS Code Web, use **Sync Changes** or **Push** (from the **...** menu) in Source Control.

Then return to the repository page on GitHub and confirm the README change appears there.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-push-change">
  <span>I pushed or synced the commit to GitHub.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-confirm-github">
  <span>I found the updated README on GitHub.</span>
</label>

:::

Useful terminal reference:

```bash
git push
```


## 6. Mark A Shared Version

Now pretend this repository contains a draft you are sending to coauthors.

In Source Control:

1. Find the **Graph** section.
1. Right-click the most recent commit.
1. Choose **Create Tag...**.
1. Name the tag `2026-06-03-to-MP`, or substitute "MP" with the initials of your imaginary coauthor.
1. Confirm the tag.

Then push the tag to GitHub from the terminal:

```bash
git push origin 2026-06-03-to-MP
```

Return to the repository page on GitHub and confirm that the tag appears.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-create-tag">
  <span>I created a tag from the Source Control graph.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-confirm-tag">
  <span>I found the tag on GitHub.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2a-understand-tag">
  <span>I can explain why a tag is useful when sending a draft or submitting a paper.</span>
</label>

:::

:::
